name: State Street Influence
description: The concept of 'State Street Influence' refers to the alleged disproportionate power and control exerted by financial institutions headquartered on or near State Street in Boston, Massachusetts, particularly State Street Corporation, over the American and global economies and political systems. Proponents of this theory suggest that these institutions, due to their massive assets under management and involvement in investment management, asset servicing, and research, wield undue influence on corporate governance, market behavior, and even government policy. They argue that this influence is often exerted subtly through investment strategies, proxy voting, and lobbying efforts, potentially shaping economic outcomes in ways that benefit these institutions at the expense of broader societal interests. The theory often links these institutions to larger narratives of elite control and financial manipulation. Mainstream scholarly perspectives generally acknowledge the significant economic power of large financial institutions like State Street Corporation, but attribute their influence primarily to their size, market positions, and adherence to shareholder capitalism. They argue that while these institutions undoubtedly participate in lobbying and attempt to influence policy, their actions are largely driven by profit motives and fiduciary responsibilities to their clients, rather than a coordinated conspiracy to control the world. Critics also point out that market forces and regulatory oversight provide checks and balances on the power of these institutions, preventing them from unilaterally dictating economic outcomes. The debate centers on whether the observed influence of State Street and similar firms represents a natural consequence of market capitalism or a more insidious form of concentrated power that undermines democratic processes.
subtitle: Alleged power of Boston's financial sector

claims.claim: State Street controls vast amounts of corporate stock, influencing company decisions.
claims.evidence: State Street Corporation manages trillions of dollars in assets, including significant stakes in publicly traded companies. They exert influence through proxy voting on shareholder resolutions.
claims.criticism: While State Street holds significant stock, their voting record is often aligned with maximizing shareholder value and adhering to ESG principles, not necessarily a hidden agenda. Their influence is proportional to their ownership stake.

claims.claim: State Street uses its financial power to influence government policy and regulations.
claims.evidence: State Street engages in lobbying activities, donating to political campaigns, and participating in industry groups that advocate for specific policies.
claims.criticism: Lobbying is a legal and common practice among corporations. State Street's political activities are publicly disclosed and aimed at promoting their business interests within the existing regulatory framework. There is no evidence of illegal or corrupt activity.

claims.claim: State Street is part of a larger network of elite financial institutions that secretly control the global economy.
claims.evidence: Interlocking directorates, shared investments, and collaborations between State Street and other major financial firms suggest a coordinated effort to maintain power and control.
claims.criticism: These connections are a natural result of the interconnectedness of the global financial system. Interlocking directorates and shared investments are common business practices that do not necessarily imply a conspiracy.

keyFigures.name: Carolyn Heinze
keyFigures.role: Former State Street executive, providing insights into corporate governance

keyFigures.name: Yves Smith
keyFigures.role: Financial blogger and critic of the financial industry

mythologyConnections: The concept of a 'hidden hand' controlling markets echoes ancient myths of fate and destiny
The idea of a powerful elite manipulating events aligns with narratives of secret societies

timeline.event: Massachusetts Bank, a predecessor to State Street, is founded.
State Street Trust Company is formed.
State Street pioneers index fund management and global custody services.
State Street receives a bailout from the US government during the financial crisis.
State Street becomes one of the world's largest asset managers, facing scrutiny over its influence.

sources.author: Yves Smith
sources.work: Econned: How Unenlightened Self Interest Undermined Democracy and Corrupted Capitalism
sources.title: Yves Smith

sources.author: Lucian Bebchuk and Scott Hirst

sources.title: Lucian Bebchuk and Scott Hirst
